content.full_text:
JOYCE A. PADLESCHAT  
ATTORNEY  
801 SOUTH GRAND AVENUE  
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90017  
(213) 895-5184

June 5, 1990

John Rothman, Esquire  
Fair Political Practices Commission  
Post Office Box 807  
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Mr. Rothman:

This letter is intended to confirm our telephone conversation of Friday, June 1, in which you advised that publication of the PAC newsletter article described below did not constitute a reportable independent expenditure or a contribution.

The article generally presented the arguments for and against Propositions 108 and 111 and stated that the corporation which sponsors the PAC supports the two ballot measures. It did not urge the reader's support. The PAC newsletter which contained the article was printed and distributed as regularly scheduled; no additional costs were incurred due to the article. The PAC newsletter is distributed only to managerial employees and shareholders.

In responding to my question about the need to report costs associated with the article, you referred me to regulation 18225 (b) (4) (C). That section states that costs incurred for a regularly published newsletter whose circulation is limited to an organization's members, employees, shareholders and other affiliated individuals are not "expenditures". This exception also assumes that there were no additional costs above those normally incurred for the newsletter's publication. Thus, the PAC newsletter described above would not be a reportable "expenditure".

I also asked if the article could be considered a contribution, particularly if the sponsoring corporation received general requests from the ballot measure committee to help promote the two propositions. I referenced regulation 18225 (c) which states that any payment used to make a contribution is an expenditure.

John Rothman, Esquire  
June 5, 1990  
Page 2

You responded to my second question in two parts. First, the article would not be considered to have been made at the "behest" of the ballot measure committee if the committee's request was general, not directed at the corporation and was the same request given to many persons at the same time. Second, you stated that if a payment is a contribution, then it is also an expenditure; if it is not an expenditure, then it is also not a contribution.

Please let me know if I have accurately reflected our conversation. Incidentally, I sincerely appreciate the help you have given me on this issue and on many issues in the past.

Very truly yours,

Joyce A. Padleschat
